Create a Theme

A complete walkthrough for building an Outpost theme from scratch — from an empty directory to a fully functioning site with pages, a blog, and custom content fields.

Before you start

Make sure Outpost is running and you can access the admin panel. You will be creating files in the outpost/themes/ directory on your server (or local environment). You do not need to restart PHP or rebuild anything — Outpost picks up new theme files automatically on the next request.

Step 1: Create the directory structure

Choose a short, lowercase, hyphenated slug for your theme. This becomes the directory name and is used internally everywhere. Create the required subdirectories:

mkdir -p outpost/themes/my-theme/partials
mkdir outpost/themes/my-theme/assets

At this point your theme directory looks like:

outpost/themes/my-theme/
  partials/
  assets/

Step 2: Create theme.json

Every theme requires a theme.json at its root. Without it, Outpost will not recognize the directory as a theme.

{
  "name": "My Theme",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "author": "Your Name",
  "description": "Clean and minimal."
}

See theme.json reference for all available fields.

Step 3: Create partials/head.html

The head.html partial is the standard place for the HTML document opening — DOCTYPE, <html>, <head>, meta tags, CSS links, and the opening <body> tag. It is included at the top of every page template.

Note the use of {{ meta.title }}My Site{{ /meta.title }} and {{ meta.description }}{{ /meta.description }} — these read per-page SEO values set in the admin. The content between the opening and closing tags is the default fallback used when no value has been entered. The GA4 block is conditional — it only renders the tracking snippet if the ga4_id global field has been filled in.

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html lang="en">
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
  <title>{{ meta.title }}My Site{{ /meta.title }}</title>
  <meta name="description" content="{{ meta.description }}{{ /meta.description }}">
  <meta property="og:title" content="{{ meta.title }}My Site{{ /meta.title }}">
  <meta property="og:description" content="{{ meta.description }}{{ /meta.description }}">
  {% if @og_image %}<meta property="og:image" content="{{ @og_image | image }}">{% endif %}
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="/outpost/themes/my-theme/assets/style.css">
  {% if @ga4_id %}
  <script async src="https://www.googletagmanager.com/gtag/js?id={{ @ga4_id }}"></script>
  <script>
    window.dataLayer = window.dataLayer || [];
    function gtag(){dataLayer.push(arguments);}
    gtag('js', new Date()); gtag('config', '{{ @ga4_id }}');
  </script>
  {% endif %}
</head>
<body>
Important — absolute paths only
Asset paths like /outpost/themes/my-theme/assets/style.css must start with /. Relative paths (assets/style.css) will work on the homepage but break on every other URL (/about, /post/hello-world, etc.) because the browser resolves them relative to the current path. Always use absolute paths for CSS, JS, images, and fonts.
Note — The theme slug in asset paths is the directory name, not the name field in theme.json. When you rename or duplicate a theme, update these paths in your partials.
Tip — use {% seo %} instead of manual meta tags
The head.html above uses manual {{ meta.title }} and {{ meta.description }} tags. For most sites, the {% seo %} tag is a better choice — it generates <title>, <meta description>, Open Graph, Twitter Card, canonical URL, JSON-LD, and pagination links automatically with a built-in fallback chain. Replace the manual meta tags with a single line: 
<head>
  <meta charset="UTF-8">
  <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">
  {% seo %}
  <link rel="stylesheet" href="/outpost/themes/my-theme/assets/style.css">
</head>
See SEO for the full list of tags {% seo %} generates.

Step 4: Create partials/nav.html

The navigation partial renders the site's main menu. The {% for item in menu.main %} loop iterates over items in the navigation menu named main, which you configure in Admin → Navigation. Each item exposes item.url, item.label, and item.target.

<nav class="site-nav">
  <a href="/" class="nav-logo">{{ @site_name }}</a>
  <ul class="nav-links">
  {% for item in menu.main %}
    <li><a href="{{ item.url }}" target="{{ item.target }}">{{ item.label }}</a></li>
  {% endfor %}
  </ul>
</nav>

The {{ @site_name }} tag reads the site_name global field. Global fields are set in Admin → Global Fields and are available on every page.

Step 5: Create partials/footer.html

The footer partial closes the page. It must include the closing </body> and </html> tags. This is also where you put any deferred JavaScript includes.

<footer class="site-footer">
  <p>{{ @footer_text }}</p>
  <p>&copy; {{ @site_name }}</p>
</footer>
<script src="/outpost/themes/my-theme/assets/main.js"></script>
</body>
</html>

Step 6: Create index.html

The homepage template. It includes the three standard partials and defines the page's own content fields. Here we use {{ hero_title }}Welcome{{ /hero_title }} with a fallback default, and {{ hero_subtitle | textarea }} to register the field as a multi-line text area in the admin.

{% include 'head' %}
{% include 'nav' %}

<main class="page-home">
  <section class="hero">
    <h1>{{ hero_title }}Welcome{{ /hero_title }}</h1>
    <p>{{ hero_subtitle | textarea }}</p>
    {% if hero_button_label %}
    <a href="{{ hero_button_url | link }}" class="btn">{{ hero_button_label }}</a>
    {% endif %}
  </section>

  <section class="intro">
    {{ intro_body | raw }}
  </section>
</main>

{% include 'footer' %}

Step 7: Activate the theme

Go to Admin → Themes. Your new theme should appear in the grid. Click Activate. Outpost will immediately scan all template files in the theme and create database stubs for every field it finds. You will not see errors if fields have no content yet — they simply output empty strings.

Tip — If your theme does not appear in the admin after creating theme.json, double-check that the JSON is valid. A missing comma or trailing comma will prevent it from being parsed.

Step 8: Add content in the admin

With the theme active, navigate to Admin → Pages → Home. You will see all the fields from index.html listed and ready to fill in: hero_title, hero_subtitle, hero_button_label, hero_button_url, and intro_body. Fill them in, save, and visit your site's homepage.

Global fields like site_name and footer_text are managed in Admin → Global Fields. They are shared across all pages and all templates.

Adding a blog with collections

A blog requires two templates: a list page (blog.html) and a single post page (post.html). First create the collection in Admin → Collections with slug post and URL pattern /post/{slug}. Then create the templates.

blog.html — the post list

{% include 'head' %}
{% include 'nav' %}

<main class="page-blog">
  <h1>{{ page_title }}Blog{{ /page_title }}</h1>
  <div class="post-list">
  {% for post in collection.post %}
    <article class="post-card">
      <a href="{{ post.url }}">
        {% if post.featured_image %}
        <img src="{{ post.featured_image }}" alt="{{ post.title }}">
        {% endif %}
        <h2>{{ post.title }}</h2>
        <p>{{ post.excerpt }}</p>
      </a>
    </article>
  {% endfor %}
  </div>
</main>

{% include 'footer' %}

post.html — the single post

The {% single post from collection.post %} block reads the current URL's slug, looks up the matching post, and makes its fields available as post.field_name. The {% else %} block handles the 404 case where no matching post is found.

{% include 'head' %}
{% include 'nav' %}

{% single post from collection.post %}
<main class="page-post">
  <article>
    <h1>{{ post.title }}</h1>
    <p class="post-meta">By {{ post.author }}</p>
    {% if post.featured_image %}
    <img src="{{ post.featured_image }}" alt="{{ post.title }}" class="post-hero">
    {% endif %}
    <div class="post-body">{{ post.body | raw }}</div>
  </article>
</main>
{% else %}
<main><p>Post not found.</p></main>
{% endsingle %}

{% include 'footer' %}

Adding static pages

Each page on your site is a separate .html template file. Creating about.html makes the /about URL available. Creating contact.html makes /contact available. There is no registration step — the template file is the page.

{% include 'head' %}
{% include 'nav' %}

<main class="page-about">
  <h1>{{ page_title }}About Us{{ /page_title }}</h1>
  <div class="about-body">{{ about_body | raw }}</div>
</main>

{% include 'footer' %}

After creating this file and loading /about once, Admin → Pages will show an "About" page entry with the page_title and about_body fields ready to edit.

Adding a 404 page

Create 404.html in your theme root. Outpost renders this template whenever a URL cannot be matched to any page, collection item, or template file. If 404.html does not exist, Outpost falls back to a plain built-in 404 response.

{% include 'head' %}
{% include 'nav' %}

<main class="page-404">
  <h1>Page not found</h1>
  <p>The page you are looking for does not exist.</p>
  <a href="/">Go home</a>
</main>

{% include 'footer' %}
Note — The 404.html template has no editable page fields in the admin. It is a static template. You can use globals ({{ @site_name }}, etc.) but there is no associated page record in the database for a 404 page.

Common gotchas

These are the most common mistakes developers make when building their first Outpost theme. Read this section before you start — it will save you debugging time.

Links must use route paths, not file names

Outpost's front-router maps URLs to template files, but visitors never see the file names. When writing links in your templates, always use the route path — the URL as it appears in the browser — not the template filename.

CorrectWrongWhy
<a href="/"><a href="index.html">index.html is the template file, / is the URL
<a href="/about"><a href="about.html">about.html is not a valid URL — it returns a 404
<a href="/blog"><a href="blog.html">Same pattern — use the route, not the file
<a href="/contact"><a href="contact.html">The router only matches /contact, not /contact.html

This applies to all <a href> links in your templates, partials, and any inline HTML. If you are converting a static HTML site to an Outpost theme, you must update all internal links from .html file references to route paths.

Warning — This is the #1 cause of broken links in new themes. Relative links like about.html will 404 because the front-router does not serve .html files directly — it maps clean URLs like /about to template files internally.

Asset paths must be absolute

CSS, JavaScript, images, and fonts must use absolute paths starting with /. A relative path like assets/style.css resolves differently depending on the current URL:

  • On / → browser requests /assets/style.css — wrong path, but might work by accident
  • On /about → browser requests /about/assets/style.css — 404
  • On /post/hello-world → browser requests /post/hello-world/assets/style.css — 404

The correct path is always /outpost/themes/{your-theme-slug}/assets/style.css. See Assets for details.

Collection templates need admin setup first

Unlike pages (where creating about.html instantly makes /about work), collections require two steps:

  1. Create the collection in the admin — go to Admin → Collections → New Collection. Set the name, slug (e.g. post), and URL pattern (e.g. /post/{slug}). Add any custom fields to the schema.
  2. Create the template files — create blog.html for the list page and post.html for the single item page. The single template filename must match the collection slug: a collection with slug post needs post.html, a collection with slug portfolio needs portfolio.html.

If you create the template first without the collection, the {% for item in collection.post %} loop will simply render nothing (no error, no items). If you create the collection without the template, visiting a collection item's URL will return a 404.

How field auto-scaffolding works

Every time Outpost compiles a template for the first time — or after you modify it — it scans all {{ field_name }} tags and registers any fields that do not yet exist. This means:

  • Adding a new field to a template makes it appear in the admin after the next page load.
  • Removing a field from a template makes it disappear from the admin editor. The data is not deleted — it is simply not shown. Add the tag back and the data returns.
  • Changing a field's filter (e.g., from plain text to | textarea) updates how it is displayed in the admin editor.

Theme duplication for safe experimentation

When you want to make significant changes to a live theme, the safest approach is to duplicate it first. In Admin → Themes, click the Duplicate button on the active theme. This creates a full copy — all templates, partials, and assets — under a new slug (e.g., my-theme-v2). Activate the duplicate and make your changes there. The original theme remains untouched. If something goes wrong, switch back to the original immediately.

Tip — Theme duplication copies files, but the content in the database (page fields, global fields, collection items) is shared across all themes. Activating a different theme does not lose any content — it just changes which template files are used to render it.
